main_sections.title: Vitamin-M
main_sections.prose_summary: Vitamin-M is a gently comic yet deeply humane short story by Asha Nehemiah about the tensions and tenderness that arise when an elderly grandparent moves into a city flat. The narrative opens with Ravi's mother wishing someone would invent 'Vitamin-M' — a pill to improve old people's memories — unaware that her 75-year-old father, Grandpa, can hear her from his rocking chair. Grandpa has come to live with the family after a series of alarming incidents at his beloved small-town home: an accidental double dose of medicines led to hospitalisation, and a fall while pottering in his garden left him lying outside all night. Ravi's mother, Vidya, has locked up the house and brought him to their 'poky little flat' in the city against his wishes. Grandpa resents the loss of independence — he had managed house, cooking, and shopping alone for ten years after his wife's death — and bristles at being treated like a child. The central episode unfolds on the first day of Ravi's school holidays. After his mother leaves for work and instructs Ravi to keep Grandpa at home, Grandpa craftily manoeuvres a confession from Ravi that he has received no such instruction, then strides out with his mahogany walking stick (brass handle carved as an eagle's head) and yellow cap. Ravi is caught in a dilemma — he cannot accompany Grandpa openly without hurting his dignity, nor can he let him roam unsupervised. He follows Grandpa secretly, a comedy of errors ensuing: crouching behind an elephant-shaped bush in the children's park earns him the wrath of a mother and a crawl through the park on all fours; hiding behind a banyan tree at a tea stall provokes loud vendor women who mistake him for a new hawker; ducking into a shop next to the barber's lands him in a Ladies' Hairdressing Salon from which he is evicted amid shrieks. Grandpa, meanwhile, enjoys a sugary tea, two bananas, and an ice cream — all banned at home — and gives his yellow cap to a bald stranger at the barber shop as a generous gesture. Ravi, fooled by the stranger's cap and white clothes, boards a bus chasing the wrong man, and by the time he returns home, Grandpa is already safely asleep. The story concludes with a twist of wit: Grandpa coolly tells Ravi's mother that he had 'a quiet morning' but that Ravi had disappeared, embarrassing Ravi completely. Grandpa then reveals birthday gifts for everyone, correcting Ravi's mother when she assumes he has forgotten Ravi's birthday — it is Grandpa's own birthday, and he always gives gifts on that day. He suggests that Ravi's mother needs Vitamin-M herself, and presents Ravi with The Best Detective Stories, winking at his amateur surveillance. The story explores themes of intergenerational dignity and respect, the invisibility and infantilisation of the elderly, selective memory (Grandpa's chess knowledge is encyclopaedic though he forgets names), the tension between safety and autonomy, and the quiet love between grandparent and grandchild. The embedded story 'The Lost Child' by Mulk Raj Anand (pp.120-125) appears as a *Learning Beyond the Text* supplementary reading and is not a separate main section.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Reflect and Respond + Reading for Meaning + Check Your Understanding + Critical Reflection

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I From your experience with an elderly person at home or in your neighbourhood, complete the following graphic organiser. Now, discuss the points written in the organiser with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: Why should we care for the elderly?
What are the problems faced by the elderly?
What could be the possible reasons for the problems faced?
How can we take care of the elderly?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: II Fill in the table given below. You may check your answers with the help of a dictionary. [Words: frail, shuddered, poky, forbid, pottering, winced — columns: Not heard this word before / Have heard this word but don't know what it means / I know this word. It has something to do with… / Write the meaning of this word]

question_bank.stem: frail
shuddered
poky
forbid
pottering
winced

tasks_verbatim.task_text: III Think of a tradition, story, or recipe passed down by grandparents or an elderly relative in your family. What does it mean to you? How does it connect you to your growing-up years? Share with your classmates and teacher.

Check Your Understanding [after Reading for Meaning section I, p.97–102] — Answer the following questions. I Why did Grandpa dislike living in the city with his daughter? II Why did Vidya not want her father to go out alone? III Was Ravi in favour of his mother treating Grandpa like a child? How can you say so? IV Would Ravi be able to keep up his promise to his mother?

question_bank.stem: Why did Grandpa dislike living in the city with his daughter?
Why did Vidya not want her father to go out alone?
Was Ravi in favour of his mother treating Grandpa like a child? How can you say so?
Would Ravi be able to keep up his promise to his mother?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Check Your Understanding [after Reading for Meaning section II, p.102–106] — I Complete the flow chart by describing Grandpa's day out. [Flow chart with 7 blanks: 1.first stop, 2.buys/watches, 3.next goes to, 4.scolded by, 5.drinks/eats, 6.enters, 7.walks quickly to/boards]

question_bank.stem: Complete the flow chart by describing Grandpa's day out (7 blanks).
question_bank.table:
Blank|Description
1|Grandpa first stops at ___
2|buys ___ and watches ___
3|Grandpa next goes to ___
4|Ravi gets scolded by ___ and ___
5|Grandpa drinks ___ and eats ___
6|Grandpa then crosses the road and enters a ___
7|Next Grandpa walks quickly to a ___ and ___

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Check Your Understanding [after Reading for Meaning section II, cont.] — II Was Grandpa lost as feared by Ravi's mother? III How would Ravi feel after seeing Grandpa? IV Do you think Ravi's mother will get to know about what had happened?

question_bank.stem: Was Grandpa lost as feared by Ravi's mother?
How would Ravi feel after seeing Grandpa?
Do you think Ravi's mother will get to know about what had happened?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Check Your Understanding [after Reading for Meaning section III, p.107–109] — I Describe how Ravi's emotional reaction upon finding Grandpa safe at home reveals his feelings towards his grandfather. II What might be the significance of Grandpa's habit of giving gifts to everyone on his birthday rather than receiving them? III Why do you think Grandpa gave Ravi a detective story book as a gift? What might this suggest about Grandpa's awareness of Ravi's actions earlier in the day?

question_bank.stem: Describe how Ravi's emotional reaction upon finding Grandpa safe at home reveals his feelings towards his grandfather.
What might be the significance of Grandpa's habit of giving gifts to everyone on his birthday rather than receiving them?
Why do you think Grandpa gave Ravi a detective story book as a gift? What might this suggest about Grandpa's awareness of Ravi's actions earlier in the day?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Critical Reflection — I Read the extracts given below and answer the questions that follow. Extract 1: 'Grandpa hated the noise and bustle of city life and when they were on their own, he often spoke longingly of his small brick house in town. "Such a wonderful place…! With that big mango tree in the garden! It is so quiet that at dusk you can even hear a leaf fall!" But then Grandpa had slipped and fallen in the garden late one evening while he was pottering around and lain outside all night because there was no one at home to help him up.'

question_bank.stem: Choose the emotion displayed by the Grandfather. A. regretful B. apologetic C. nostalgic D. wistful
Complete the following sentence with a reason. Grandpa hated the busy and noisy city life because ___________.
Why did Grandpa come to the city despite his dislike for city life?
Choose an expression similar in meaning to 'you can even hear a leaf fall'. A. end of one's life B. arrival of autumn C. sign of good luck D. pin drop silence
State one advantage and one disadvantage of the Grandfather living in the house in town.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Critical Reflection — I Extract 2: '"I had a quiet morning, but I don't know about Ravi. He just disappeared instead of staying at home to look after me like you told him to," answered Grandpa coolly, while Ravi just looked confused and embarrassed. Grandpa had another surprise for Ravi. A gift-wrapped parcel! "But, Papa, Ravi's birthday was three months ago. Have you forgotten?" exclaimed Ravi's mother, impatiently.'

question_bank.stem: Choose the option that displays the tone of the Grandfather's response. A. apologetic B. hurtful C. calm D. puzzled
Why was Ravi confused and embarrassed?
Complete the sentence with a suitable reason. Ravi's mother was impatient because ________________.
State whether the following sentence is true or false. Grandfather forgot that Ravi's birthday was three months ago.
Why did Grandpa say he didn't know what kind of morning Ravi might have had?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Critical Reflection — II Answer the following questions. 1. Grandpa is portrayed as a person with failing memory in the beginning of the story. Give two evidences to disprove the statement. 2. Give two characteristic traits of each of the following characters: (i) Grandfather (ii) Ravi (iii) Ravi's mother. 3. Why was Ravi worried about what Grandfather had at the tea stall? 4. Do you think it was easy for Ravi to follow his Grandfather? If yes, why? If no, why not? 5. 'Ravi is a good detective'. Give one argument in favour of and against the given statement. 6. Was Grandfather aware that Ravi was following him? How do you know? 7. In your opinion, who needs Vitamin-M? And why? 8. Some people believe society shows bias based on age. Give an example where you might have experienced or observed such discrimination, when opinions were disregarded because of age.

question_bank.stem: Grandpa is portrayed as a person with failing memory in the beginning of the story. Give two evidences to disprove the statement.
Give two characteristic traits of each of the following characters: (i) Grandfather (ii) Ravi (iii) Ravi's mother.
Why was Ravi worried about what Grandfather had at the tea stall?
Do you think it was easy for Ravi to follow his Grandfather? If yes, why? If no, why not?
'Ravi is a good detective'. Give one argument in favour of and against the given statement.
Was Grandfather aware that Ravi was following him? How do you know?
In your opinion, who needs Vitamin-M? And why?

Some people believe society shows bias based on age (being young or old). Give an example where you might have experienced or observed such discrimination, when opinions were disregarded because of age.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I Classify the words given in the box into movement words in Column 1 and sound words in Column 2. One example has been done for you. [Words: pottering, boomed, twirling, crouch, bustle, crawl, creeping, thudded, ducked, shrieks, zigzagging, whirr, evicted, briskly, sprinting, grunted, jumping, darted, trailing, snoring] Now, fill in the blanks with appropriate words from Column 1 or Column 2. [Detective Shankar story fill-in, blanks 1–10]

question_bank.stem: Classify movement vs. sound words (Column 1 and Column 2).
Fill in the blanks with appropriate movement/sound words in the Detective Shankar passage (10 blanks).

tasks_verbatim.task_text: II Study the highlighted word in the given sentence from the text and fill in the boxes with synonymous words. You may refer to a dictionary. 'Off he went, twirling his walking stick jauntily, leaving Ravi in a dilemma.' [Find two synonyms: 1. QU_ND_ _Y = QUANDARY, 2. PR_D_C_M_NT = PREDICAMENT]

question_bank.stem: Find synonyms for DILEMMA: 1. QU_ ND_ _ Y and 2. PR_ D _ C _ M _ NT

tasks_verbatim.task_text: III Fill in the table given below. Check your answers with the help of a dictionary. [Words: craftily, dilemma, furious, boomed, humiliation, attire — columns: Meaning, Part of Speech, Synonym, Antonym]

tasks_verbatim.task_text: IV Match the emotions/expressions in Column 1 with their meanings in Column 2. One meaning is extra. [Column 1: embarrassed, countered, flushed, stricken, gleam — Column 2: (i) affected severely by an unpleasant feeling, (ii) to be confused, (iii) expression of emotion, (iv) reply to an argument, (v) felt uncomfortable, (vi) became red in the face]

tasks_verbatim.task_text: V Read the highlighted words in the following phrases from the story. Now, revisit the story and underline the prepositions. [Phrases: 1. walk across to the corner shop, 2. shadow fall over him, 3. vendors sitting under the tree, 4. Ravi took position behind a big banyan] Now, fill in the blanks with appropriate prepositions. [Passage with blanks (i)–(vi) choosing from given options: through/between/against; over/under/beside; among/near/across; on/at/in; beside/through/along; into/towards/beyond]

question_bank.stem: Underline the prepositions in the four given phrases from the story.
Fill in the blanks with appropriate prepositions (i)–(vi) in the given passage.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: VI Read the following dialogues from the story [5 dialogues given]. Now, choose the correct answer. A. The words in the quotes are (i) ___ (exclamatory/interrogative) sentences. B. Sentences 1, 4, and 5 are (ii) ___ (Wh-/Yes, No type) interrogative sentences. C. Sentences 2 and 3 are (iii) ___ (Wh-/Yes, No type) interrogative sentences. So, there are (iv) ___ (two/three) types of interrogative sentences. [Grammar note on direct vs. indirect speech with transformation table; D. Read the following conversation between Arvind and Priya and complete the sentences in reported speech (6 blanks).]

question_bank.stem: Choose the correct answer: (i) The words in the quotes are ___ (exclamatory/interrogative) sentences.
Sentences 1, 4, and 5 are (ii) ___ (Wh-/Yes, No type) interrogative sentences.
Sentences 2 and 3 are (iii) ___ (Wh-/Yes, No type) interrogative sentences.
So, there are (iv) ___ (two/three) types of interrogative sentences.
Complete the following sentences in reported speech [Arvind–Priya conversation, 6 blanks].

listening.transcript_text: Dear students, welcome to today's session on meditation. Meditation is an important yogic practice commonly used for relaxation and stress management. Basically, meditation provides rest and peace to the mind. During meditation, all kinds of thoughts which cause stress are removed, resulting in an enhanced state of well-being, both physically and emotionally. After meditation, the person feels fresh and gains a new perspective on stressful situations. Meditation also helps to reduce negative emotions and increases self-awareness. I encourage you to start practising meditation on a regular basis and make it your routine — it will contribute to your overall well-being and positively influence your memory power. In fact, practising meditation regularly can help lessen exam-related pressure, allowing you to manage challenges with ease. So, let us begin the journey of meditation together.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I You will listen to a podcast on meditation. As you listen, complete the sentences with not more than three exact words that you hear. (Transcript for teacher on page 266) 1. Meditation is a form of yoga that usually refreshes the mind and helps with ___. 2. Meditation leads to a better health condition both ___. 3. Meditation also aids in lessening undesirable feelings and improving ___. 4. It is advised that we meditate every day so that it becomes a ___. 5. Meditating on a daily basis is beneficial in comfortably tackling ___.

question_bank.stem: Meditation is a form of yoga that usually refreshes the mind and helps with ___.
Meditation leads to a better health condition both ___.
Meditation also aids in lessening undesirable feelings and improving ___.
It is advised that we meditate every day so that it becomes a ___.
Meditating on a daily basis is beneficial in comfortably tackling ___.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I Intonation refers to the pitch of the voice while speaking. In questions, intonation can indicate the sentiment or the expected response. [Examples given for falling intonation — Wh/How questions; rising intonation — Yes/No questions; rising intonation — affirmative main clause + negative question tag; falling intonation — negative main clause + affirmative question tag; rise-fall intonation — choice questions.] Now, revisit the story and select different questions. Work in pairs and practise speaking them aloud according to the examples shared.

II Make a presentation about a brief personal experience related to taking care of an older person. For example, mention a specific individual you cared for or an occasion when you provided assistance to that person. You may use the following prompts to structure your narration: Background of your experience; Reflect on what you learned about yourself during this caregiving experience; Understanding the needs and preferences of the older person; Their challenges offered insights into the difficulties of aging.

I As you read in the story, we have a lot to learn from our elderly. Based on the lessons learned from them, write an article for your school magazine on the topic 'Our Inspiring Elderly'. Organise the article in the manner given below: Paragraph 1 — brief introduction; Paragraph 2 — details and examples of how the elderly inspire; Paragraph 3 — how they overcame challenges and their inspiring impact; Paragraph 4 — concluding comment. Write the title at the top and your name and grade below it.

I Revisit the story and choose the image that correctly displays Grandpa's walking stick. [Four images of walking sticks shown — the correct one has a brass handle carved in the shape of an eagle's head.]

II A picture is worth a thousand words! Explore and revisit memories. Collect a few memorable photographs of yourself and your family. Make a slide show. Mention the following: When and where was it clicked? Who are the people in the photograph? Why is it memorable for you?

III You must have read stories about taking care of family members of all age groups in English and also in your own language. Now, read the story given below. Discuss with your classmates and teacher. [Story: 'The Lost Child' by Mulk Raj Anand — about a young child who gets separated from his parents at a spring festival fair; he had eagerly wanted toys, sweets, balloons, flowers, and a ride on the roundabout, but each time refrained knowing his parents would refuse. Once lost, he rejects all the same attractions offered by a kind stranger, crying only for his mother and father. The story contrasts desire suppressed by parental authority against the overwhelming bond of attachment when that security is lost.]
